title: Tahitian: when one word denotes an activity/state, its product/result, or the institution/object that enables it
introduction: Across the data, Tahitian shows a robust metonymic flexibility whereby a single form can cover (i) an activity or state, and (ii) either the concrete product/result of that activity or the institution/occasion/object that enables it. Two mechanisms are visible. First, category-flexible roots: the same root is freely used predicatively (as a verb/stative) and nominally (as a product/result item). Clear cases include **hīmene** ‘sing/SONG’, **'ori** ‘dance/DANCE (piece)’, **tāmā'a** ‘eat/MEAL’, **mana'o** ‘think/IDEA; opinion’, **māuiui** ‘be ill/ILLNESS’. Second, the suffixal nominalizer **-ra'a**, which forms action/event nouns. These **-ra'a** nouns highlight either an event/occasion or, by extension, the organized activity or institution that enables performances (e.g., **hīmenera'a** ‘singing (as an event)’, **'orira'a** ‘dancing (as an event)’, **ha'utira'a** ‘(their) games/playing’, **fa'aipoipora'a** ‘wedding’, **mōmo'ara'a** ‘engagement’, **fa'anahora'a** ‘organization’). The same metonymic alternations are attested across domains: arts, daily life, institutional life, mental life, and bodily states. Collocation and morphology—especially **-ra'a**, compounding, and frames like possession or selection—guide which reading (activity vs product/enabler) is intended.

sections.focus: Category‑flexible roots: activity/state vs product/result noun
sections.description:
Roots can function as predicates (activities/states) or as nouns naming the product/resulting item. The intended reading is resolved by syntactic frame and collocation.
- Tahitian: e hīmene mai tō mahina i tā rātou mau hīmene, 'e 'ori ato'a mai tā rātou 'ori
  English: The people of X will sing their songs, dance their dances;
  Explanation: The same roots supply activity and product: **hīmene** ‘to sing’ vs **hīmene** ‘songs’; **'ori** ‘to dance’ vs **'ori** ‘dances (pieces)’. Possession and plural marker on **tā rātou mau hīmene/'ori** favor the product/set reading.
- Tahitian: e ma'iti tātou i te 'ori e au 'ia fa'a'ite
  English: We must choose dances to showcase
  Explanation: Selection syntax (**e ma'iti**) forces **'ori** to mean dance items (pieces), not the action.
- Tahitian: i muri iho, e tāmā'a 'āmui pauroa mai tātou
  English: After that, we’ll all share lunch together.
  Explanation: **tāmā'a** can denote the meal (product/occasion of eating) as well as the activity ‘to eat’ elsewhere. Context ‘share lunch’ triggers the meal reading.
- Tahitian: 'ā, mana'o maita'i
  English: Yes, good idea.
  Explanation: **mana'o** denotes a concrete idea/opinion (product of thinking) in contrast to its verbal/stative use ‘to think’ in other contexts.
- Tahitian: 'e e māuiui ato'a te 'ōpū
  English: And also, my belly hurts.
  Explanation: Predicative **māuiui** describes a bodily state (‘be sick/in pain’); elsewhere it also names illnesses as nouns (see below for disease labels like **māuiui 'ōpū** ‘belly‑ache’).**

Key point: In all these cases, the same root alternates between denoting an ongoing activity/state and a concrete product/result item associated with that activity.

sections.focus: The nominalizer -ra'a: event/occasion nouns and enabling institutions/sets
sections.description:
The suffix **-ra'a** derives action/event nouns. Depending on collocation, these can denote (i) an event/occasion, (ii) an organized activity or institution that enables the action, or (iii) a set/collection or product emerging from the activity.
- Tahitian: i te avatea, tē ti'aturi nei au ē, e tupu te hīmenera'a 'e te 'orira'a
  English: In the afternoon, I hope we can have songs and dances.
  Explanation: **hīmenera'a** and **'orira'a** are event nouns ‘singing’/‘dancing’, here naming the occasions that enable performances (an organized time for singing/dancing).
- Tahitian: hina'aro pa'i au nō tā'u ha'api'ira'a numera i teie mahana
  English: But I need it for my math class today!
  Explanation: **ha'api'ira'a** from ‘learn/teach’ denotes the organized occasion ‘class’ (an enabling event for learning).
- Tahitian: e fare ha'api'ira'a tahito, 'aita fa'ahou i teienei
  English: That was an old school that doesn’t exist anymore.
  Explanation: In compound **fare ha'api'ira'a** ‘house of learning’, the **-ra'a** noun combines with **fare** ‘house’ to name the institution/building enabling learning, i.e., ‘school’.
- Tahitian: 'e i reira e ha'uti atu ai te ta'ata i tā rātou ha'utira'a i te po'ipo'i
  English: Then, people will play in the morning.
  Explanation: **ha'utira'a** covers ‘their games/playing’—either the activity as an organized set of play or the games as items, depending on context.
- Tahitian: 'ua rave au i tā'u ha'api'ira'a i terā puta inapō i te fare tāmā'ara'a
  English: I was doing my homework on it last night in the dining room.
  Explanation: Here **ha'api'ira'a** names the product/result of learning (‘homework’). The same sentence also shows **fare tāmā'ara'a** ‘house of eating’ = ‘dining room’, i.e., a place that enables eating.

Further attestations of enabling/event readings include items like **fa'aipoipora'a** ‘wedding’, **mōmo'ara'a** ‘engagement’, and **fa'anahora'a** ‘organization’.

sections.focus: Enablers via compounding and plant→product metonymy
sections.description:
Beyond **-ra'a**, compounding and plant names illustrate ‘enabler’ and ‘producer→product’ readings.
- Tahitian: Ua ha'ama'iri 'oia i te fāri'i tiare
  English: He dropped the vase.
  Explanation: The compound **fāri'i tiare** literally ‘flower‑receiver’ names the container that enables the display/holding of flowers (an enabling object built from ‘receive’ + ‘flower’).
- Tahitian: E mā'a maita'i te 'uru
  English: The breadfruit (fruit) is good food.
  Explanation: **'uru** is used here for the edible product of the breadfruit tree, showing a plant→product shift by metonymy (context selects the ‘fruit’ reading).
- Tahitian: e rā'au tano nō te fīva 'e te māuiui 'ōpū
  English: (There is) suitable medicine for fever and belly‑ache.
  Explanation: In the compound **māuiui 'ōpū** ‘belly‑ache’, stative **māuiui** (‘be sick’) functions as a noun naming the disease—an instance of state→labeled condition (product) metonymy.

sections.focus: How readings are disambiguated in use
sections.description:
The data show consistent cues that steer interpretation:
- Morphology: **-ra'a** strongly signals event/occasion nouns; compounding with **fare** ‘house’ yields institutions/places that enable activities (e.g., **fare ha'api'ira'a**, **fare tāmā'ara'a**).
- Selection and quantification frames: Verbs like ‘choose’ (**e ma'iti**) or possession/plural marking (**tā rātou mau …**) favor product/set readings (e.g., **'ori** as dance pieces; **hīmene** as songs).
- Collocational domains: Time expressions (e.g., ‘in the afternoon’, ‘in the morning’) or venue NPs favor event/occasion readings of **-ra'a** forms (**hīmenera'a**, **'orira'a**, **ha'utira'a**); dining‑room context supports **tāmā'a** as a meal.
- Lexicalized compounds: Head + modifier compounds like **fāri'i tiare** ‘flower‑receiver (vase)’ encode enabling objects; disease compounds like **māuiui 'ōpū** ‘belly‑ache’ encode labeled conditions.

These cues interact to license either the activity/state, the resulting product, or the enabling institution/object reading with the same lexical base.

Tahitian regularly uses one form to cover an activity/state and either its product/result or the occasion/institution/object that enables it. Two central mechanisms are (i) category‑flexible roots (e.g., **hīmene**, **'ori**, **tāmā'a**, **mana'o**, **māuiui**) and (ii) the event nominalizer **-ra'a** (e.g., **hīmenera'a**, **'orira'a**, **ha'utira'a**, and compounds like **fare ha'api'ira'a**). Collocation (selection, possession, time/venue) and morphology (especially **-ra'a**, compounding with **fare**) guide the intended reading. This metonymic flexibility is attested across arts, daily life, institutions, mental life, and bodily states, and should be expected as a productive interpretive pattern in Tahitian.
